seo.title: Government Workflow Automation AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for workflow automation AI agents in government. Covers FedRAMP, FISMA, NIST 800-53.

content.intro: Deploying AI agents in government workflow automation carries extraordinary stakes — errors can trigger national security incidents, civil liberties violations, and congressional scrutiny. This checklist operationalizes FedRAMP, FISMA, NIST 800-53, and the Executive Order on AI requirements into concrete deployment gates for multi-step agentic systems. Every item is scoped to the unique risks of AI-assisted government workflows where automated decisions directly affect citizens and mission-critical operations.

sections.heading: Authorization & Compliance Gating
sections.description: Ensure the AI agent system has completed all mandatory federal authorization processes before any production workflow execution begins.

items.label: Obtain FedRAMP Authorization to Operate (ATO)
items.description: Confirm the AI agent platform and all integrated APIs hold a current FedRAMP Moderate or High ATO. Document the authorization boundary explicitly, including all LLM inference endpoints, orchestration layers, and API integration points.

items.label: Complete FISMA System Security Plan (SSP)
items.description: Register the AI workflow automation system in the agency's FISMA inventory with a completed SSP that documents all data flows, processing locations, and interconnections. Assign a formal FISMA impact level (Low/Moderate/High) based on the sensitivity of workflows being automated.

items.label: Conduct Algorithmic Impact Assessment (AIA)
items.description: Perform a documented AIA per agency AI governance policy and the Executive Order on AI before deployment. The assessment must identify which citizen-affecting decisions the agent influences, quantify error rates, and define mitigation controls for each identified risk.

items.label: Verify No Classified Data Reaches External LLMs
items.description: Implement and test technical controls — including data classification tagging, input sanitization filters, and network egress rules — that prevent any CUI, SBU, or classified data from being transmitted to external LLM APIs. Conduct a red-team test specifically targeting data exfiltration via prompt injection.

items.label: Map NIST 800-53 Controls to Agent Components
items.description: Produce a control traceability matrix mapping all applicable NIST 800-53 Rev 5 controls (particularly AC, AU, SI, and SA families) to specific agent components: the orchestrator, each API integration, the human-in-the-loop approval module, and the rollback mechanism.

items.label: Register AI System in Agency AI Inventory
items.description: Add the workflow automation agent to the agency's AI use case inventory as required by the Executive Order on AI and OMB guidance. Include use case description, impacted populations, risk tier, and responsible AI officer sign-off.

items.label: Validate Section 508 Compliance for Human-in-the-Loop Interfaces
items.description: Test all approval dashboards, decision review screens, and audit log interfaces against WCAG 2.1 AA and Section 508 standards using both automated tools (e.g., axe, WAVE) and manual testing with assistive technologies. Document remediation of all critical and serious violations before go-live.

sections.heading: Agent Action Safety & Permission Validation
sections.description: Implement technical safeguards ensuring the agent only executes authorized actions, validates permissions at runtime, and cannot be manipulated into performing out-of-scope operations.

items.label: Enforce Least-Privilege API Scopes Per Workflow Step
items.description: Assign each workflow step a distinct service account or OAuth scope with only the minimum permissions required for that specific action. The orchestration layer must validate that the requested action matches the permitted scope before execution — not just at session initiation.

items.label: Implement Pre-Execution Action Correctness Verification
items.description: Before each consequential action (record updates, approvals, notifications, data transfers), the agent must perform a structured verification step: confirm the action parameters match the triggering workflow context, validate against business rules, and log the verification result with a unique transaction ID.

items.label: Deploy Prompt Injection Detection on All External Inputs
items.description: Instrument all data ingestion points — form submissions, document uploads, API responses, email content — with prompt injection detection filters before content reaches the LLM reasoning layer. Define and test specific government-context attack patterns (e.g., injections via citizen-submitted documents).

items.label: Enforce Idempotency Keys on All State-Changing Operations
items.description: Require idempotency keys for every API call that modifies government records, triggers payments, sends official communications, or changes workflow state. Implement server-side deduplication with a minimum 24-hour idempotency window to prevent duplicate actions from retries or agent loops.

items.label: Define and Test Agent Action Boundaries with Negative Test Cases
items.description: Document an explicit allowlist of actions the agent is permitted to take per workflow type. Execute a test suite of at least 20 negative cases per workflow — including attempts to access out-of-scope records, escalate privileges, or chain actions beyond defined boundaries — and verify all are blocked and logged.

items.label: Implement Conditional Logic Audit Trail
items.description: Log every conditional branch decision the agent makes (e.g., routing logic, eligibility determinations, escalation triggers) with the input values, rule evaluated, and outcome. This log must be tamper-evident and retained per NARA records schedules to support appeals and investigations.

items.label: Validate Human-in-the-Loop Approval Cannot Be Bypassed
items.description: Conduct adversarial testing to confirm that no combination of inputs, timeouts, or error states allows the agent to proceed past a mandatory human approval gate. Implement a dead-man's switch: if approval is not received within the defined SLA, the workflow pauses and alerts the supervisor — it does not auto-approve.

sections.heading: Explainability, Appeals & Citizen Rights
sections.description: Ensure every AI-assisted decision affecting citizens is explainable in plain language, documented for audit, and supported by a formal appeals process as required by federal AI policy.

items.label: Generate Plain-Language Decision Explanations for Every Citizen-Affecting Output
items.description: Configure the agent to produce a structured explanation for each decision output that: (1) states the decision in plain language at a 6th-grade reading level, (2) lists the top 3-5 factors that influenced the outcome, and (3) identifies which data sources were used. Store this explanation linked to the decision record.

items.label: Implement Formal Appeals Workflow with Documented Escalation Path
items.description: Build an appeals intake mechanism accessible to affected citizens that captures the contested decision ID, grounds for appeal, and supporting documentation. Define and publish SLAs for human review of appeals. Ensure the appeals process is accessible via Section 508-compliant interfaces and does not require the citizen to interact with the AI agent.

items.label: Maintain Immutable Decision Audit Log Linked to Citizen Records
items.description: Store a tamper-evident log entry for every agent decision that includes: timestamp, workflow version, input data hash, model version, decision output, explanation text, and the identity of any human approver. Link this log to the relevant citizen or case record and retain per applicable NARA schedule.

items.label: Publish AI Use Notice in Plain Language at Point of Service
items.description: Display a clear, plain-language notice at every citizen touchpoint where AI-assisted decisions may occur. The notice must explain what the AI does, what data it uses, how decisions can be appealed, and who the responsible human official is. Obtain legal review of notice language before deployment.

items.label: Conduct Disparate Impact Analysis Before Go-Live
items.description: Run the agent's decision logic against representative historical case data segmented by protected class characteristics. Document the analysis methodology, results, and any identified disparate impact. If disparate impact is found, halt deployment until mitigation controls are implemented and re-tested.

items.label: Define Human Override Protocol for All Automated Decisions
items.description: Document and train staff on a formal protocol for human officials to override any agent decision. The override must be logged with the official's identity, justification, and the corrected outcome. Test that overrides propagate correctly through downstream workflow steps.

sections.heading: Rollback, Incident Response & Continuous Monitoring
sections.description: Establish operational controls to detect anomalous agent behavior, execute safe rollbacks, and respond to incidents in alignment with federal cybersecurity requirements.

items.label: Implement Per-Workflow Rollback Procedures with Tested Recovery Scripts
items.description: Define a documented rollback procedure for each workflow type that specifies: which actions are reversible, the rollback sequence, the responsible operator, and the maximum rollback window. Execute rollback drills quarterly and document results. For irreversible actions (e.g., sent official notices), define compensating controls.

items.label: Configure Real-Time Anomaly Alerting on Agent Behavior Metrics
items.description: Instrument the agent with behavioral baselines (actions per hour, error rates, approval bypass attempts, unusual API call sequences) and configure SIEM alerts for deviations exceeding defined thresholds. Route critical alerts to the SOC and the responsible AI officer within 15 minutes.

items.label: Integrate Agent Logs with Agency SIEM and FISMA Continuous Monitoring Program
items.description: Forward all agent audit logs, API call logs, decision logs, and error logs to the agency's SIEM in real time. Ensure log format is compatible with the agency's continuous monitoring dashboard and that the AI system appears as a monitored asset in the FISMA inventory.

items.label: Define AI-Specific Incident Response Playbook
items.description: Create a dedicated IR playbook for AI agent incidents covering: model hallucination producing erroneous government actions, prompt injection attacks, unauthorized data access, and runaway automation loops. Assign roles, define containment steps (including emergency agent shutdown), and specify US-CERT reporting thresholds.

items.label: Establish Model and Workflow Version Control with Change Approval Process
items.description: Require a formal change approval process — including security impact analysis and regression testing — before deploying any update to the LLM model version, prompt templates, workflow logic, or API integrations. Maintain a version history with rollback capability to any prior production version within 4 hours.

items.label: Schedule Quarterly AI Performance and Bias Reviews
items.description: Conduct quarterly reviews of agent decision accuracy, false positive/negative rates, and disparate impact metrics using production data. Present findings to the agency AI governance board. Define performance thresholds that trigger mandatory remediation or suspension of the agent.

items.label: Test Graceful Degradation and Human Fallback Under Failure Conditions
items.description: Simulate agent failure scenarios (LLM API outage, database unavailability, approval system downtime) and verify that workflows degrade gracefully to a defined human-operated fallback process without data loss or citizen harm. Document the fallback SLA and ensure staff are trained on manual procedures.

items.label: Conduct Annual Penetration Test Targeting Agent-Specific Attack Vectors
items.description: Commission an annual penetration test scoped specifically to AI agent attack surfaces: prompt injection via workflow inputs, privilege escalation through API chaining, manipulation of conditional logic, and extraction of sensitive data from agent memory or context windows. Remediate all critical and high findings before the next ATO renewal.

notes.note: All AI agent components — including the LLM inference endpoint, orchestration platform, and API integration layer — must fall within a FedRAMP-authorized boundary. Using a commercial LLM API that lacks FedRAMP authorization for any workflow touching federal data is a FISMA violation. Verify authorization status at cloud.cisa.gov/fedramp and confirm the authorization boundary explicitly covers agentic/AI workloads, as some FedRAMP authorizations predate LLM features and may not cover them.

NIST 800-53 Rev 5 introduced AI-relevant controls that directly apply to workflow automation agents. SI-7 (Software and Information Integrity) requires integrity verification of AI model inputs and outputs. AC-6 (Least Privilege) mandates that agent service accounts hold only the minimum permissions per task — not a single elevated account for all workflow steps. AU-12 (Audit Record Generation) requires that every agent action generating a government record be individually logged with sufficient detail to reconstruct the decision chain for oversight purposes.

notes.regulation: Executive Order on AI (EO 14110) + OMB M-24-10
notes.note: EO 14110 and OMB M-24-10 require agencies to designate a Chief AI Officer, maintain an AI use case inventory, and conduct pre-deployment risk assessments for AI systems that affect rights or safety. Workflow automation agents that route benefit determinations, enforcement actions, or regulatory decisions are explicitly classified as rights-impacting AI and require enhanced oversight including annual reviews, public disclosure, and documented human accountability for outcomes.

Section 508 compliance is not optional for any government-facing interface, including the human-in-the-loop approval dashboards, decision explanation displays, and appeals intake forms used in AI workflow automation. Agencies have faced enforcement actions for inaccessible digital services. Conduct testing with actual assistive technology users — not just automated scanners — and document remediation. Note that AI-generated content (explanations, notices) must also meet plain language and readability standards under the Plain Writing Act.

cta.heading: Automate Your Government Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your government AI workflow agents against FedRAMP, FISMA, and Executive Order requirements — catching compliance gaps before they become congressional hearings.
